did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning framework for melanoma detection. Specifically, it employs transfer learning-based feature extraction using pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), followed by classification with XGBoost and Logistic Regression. Explainable AI (xAI) techniques, including Grad-CAM and LIME, are used to provide visual explanations for the classification results on the HAM10000 dermatoscopic dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel explainable melanoma detection framework that integrates transfer learning-based feature extraction with XGBoost and Logistic Regression classifiers. It details specific methodology including data collection from the HAM10000 dataset, preprocessing steps (anomaly filtering, class balancing), and reports original experimental results (e.g., XGBoost accuracy of 88.62%) and visual explanations using Grad-CAM and LIME. Although published in a conference proceedings (IATMSI), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Transfer Learning-based Deep Feature Extraction with XGBoost and xAI for Melanoma Detection
journal: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Interdisciplinary Approaches in Technology and Management for Social Innovation (IATMSI)
brief_summary: This study proposes an explainable framework for melanoma detection that integrates transfer learning-based deep feature extraction with traditional machine learning classifiers. Using the HAM10000 dermatoscopic dataset, the authors preprocess images via anomaly filtering and class balancing before extracting features with a pre-trained Convolutional Neural Network. These features are then classified using Logistic Regression and XGBoost, with XGBoost achieving a superior accuracy of 88.62%. To ensure transparency, the system employs Grad-CAM for global lesion-focused explanations and LIME for local instance-level interpretability. The results demonstrate that this approach offers a reliable, interpretable, and computationally efficient solution for automated melanoma diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for detection in the binary classification task using the HAM10000 dataset.
The comparator class in the binary classification task, representing benign pigmented skin lesions from the HAM10000 dataset.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of dermoscopic images into Melanoma (malignant) and Non-Melanoma (benign) classes.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is a 'binary classification task' differentiating between Melanoma and Non-Melanoma cases using the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (malignant vs. benign) using Logistic Regression and XGBoost classifiers, with performance evaluated via accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A collection of dermoscopic images representing different types of pigmented skin lesions, sourced from Kaggle.

use.training: Used for training the Logistic Regression and XGBoost classifiers after feature extraction, noise removal, and class balancing.
use.testing: Used for evaluating the performance of the proposed framework (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To fragment the dataset into training, validation, and testing sets while maintaining class distribution.
To optimize hyperparameters for the ML classifiers (Logistic Regression and XGBoost).
To optimize hyperparameters and assure optimal performance of the classifiers.
To evaluate the classification performance, specifically identifying misclassified cases and sensitivity issues for Logistic Regression and XGBoost.

statistical_methods_used.method: Classification report metrics (Accuracy, Precision, Recall/Sensitivity, F1 score)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To quantitatively assess and compare the performance of the Logistic Regression and XGBoost models.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrix analysis without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: XGBoost demonstrated superior performance compared to Logistic Regression. LR showed limited sensitivity to melanoma cases with many false negatives. XGBoost showed better balance across classes, particularly in recall and F1 scores for non-melanoma, and moderate improvements for melanoma. The framework utilized transfer learning-based feature extraction, Isolation Forest for noise removal, and ADASYN for class balancing.

architecture_used.main_model: Transfer Learning-based Deep Feature Extraction with XGBoost and Logistic Regression
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model; base layers frozen during initial training phase to retain generic feature representations.

architecture_details.feature_extractor: Pre-trained CNN (specific architecture name not reported, referred to generally as 'pre-trained convolutional model' or 'CNN')
architecture_details.feature_extraction_method: Intermediate layer activations extracted as high-dimensional deep feature vectors, then flattened.

training_details.hyperparameters: Optimized using grid search or cross-validation methods
training_details.training_schedule: Base layers of CNN frozen; top layers adjusted to match specific feature types.

classification_or_output_head.details: Logistic Regression and XGBoost trained on flattened CNN feature embeddings.

preprocessing_techniques: Pixel normalization by rescaling values to the range [0,1]
Real-time data augmentation including brightness adjustments, flipping, rotation, zooming, and minor geometric distortions
Stratified partitioning for dataset splitting into training, validation, and testing sets
Noise removal using Isolation Forest (unsupervised anomaly detection)
Class imbalance handling using Adaptive Synthetic Sampling (ADASYN) for synthetic oversampling of minority cases

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during training to improve limited variability and reduce overfitting. Transformations included brightness adjustments, flipping, rotation, zooming, and minor geometric distortions, incorporated in a controlled manner to preserve diagnostic characteristics.

Used for class imbalance handling (synthetic oversampling) of minority cases (melanoma) via interpolations in the feature space. This was performed prior to and subsequent to outlier removal to balance class distribution.

conclusions: The proposed framework integrating transfer learning-based deep feature extraction with XGBoost achieves superior performance (88.62% accuracy) compared to Logistic Regression (83.18% accuracy) for binary melanoma detection on the HAM10000 dataset.
Logistic Regression demonstrated limited sensitivity to melanoma cases, resulting in significant false negatives, whereas XGBoost provided better class discrimination with fewer misclassifications.

The integration of Explainable AI (xAI) techniques, specifically Grad-CAM for global lesion-focused explanations and LIME for local instance-level interpretability, enhances transparency and trust in the model's decision-making process.
LIME analysis confirms that the XGBoost model relies on dermatologically relevant features such as texture, color, and structural irregularities to distinguish between malignant and benign lesions.
The use of Isolation Forest for noise removal and ADASYN for class balancing effectively addressed dataset constraints and improved feature consistency.

The study concludes that combining deep feature extraction with traditional machine learning classifiers offers a reliable, interpretable, and computationally efficient solution for automated melanoma diagnosis, addressing the 'black box' limitations of pure deep learning models.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a single dataset (HAM10000) sourced from Kaggle, lacking external validation on independent multi-center clinical datasets, which limits generalizability.

Logistic Regression demonstrated critically low sensitivity for melanoma (0.63) and a very low F1-score (0.45), indicating a high risk of false negatives and limited clinical utility for ruling out melanoma.
XGBoost also showed suboptimal sensitivity for melanoma (0.40) despite higher overall accuracy (88.62%), suggesting the model may still miss a significant portion of malignant cases.
The specific pre-trained CNN architecture used for feature extraction is not explicitly named in the text, referred to only as a 'pre-trained convolutional model', which hinders reproducibility.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) to validate the performance difference between Logistic Regression and XGBoost.
The framework relies on synthetic oversampling (ADASYN) to handle class imbalance, which may introduce synthetic bias rather than reflecting true clinical prevalence.
The study is a conference paper published in 2026, representing early-stage research without long-term clinical deployment or prospective validation results.

While xAI techniques (Grad-CAM and LIME) are used for interpretability, the paper does not quantitatively measure the faithfulness or stability of these explanations, relying instead on qualitative visual alignment with dermatological features.
